words.shortDefinition: A strategy to delay loading non-critical resources at page load time.
words.longDefinition: Resources (like images, videos, or scripts) are loaded only when they are needed, typically when they are about to enter the viewport. This reduces initial load time, bandwidth usage, and system resource consumption.
words.techContext: Implemented natively for images and iframes using the loading='lazy' attribute, or via Intersection Observer for custom components.
words.exampleSentence: By implementing lazy loading for our product gallery, we reduced the initial page weight by over 2MB.

words.shortDefinition: Ensuring that a function is only called after a certain amount of time has passed since its last call.
words.longDefinition: A programming practice used to ensure that time-consuming tasks do not fire so often that they stall the performance of a web page. It groups multiple sequential calls into a single call.
words.techContext: Commonly used for search input fields to prevent sending an API request on every single keystroke.
words.exampleSentence: We added debouncing to the search bar so the API is only hit once the user has stopped typing for 300 milliseconds.
words.etymology: from 'bounce' (to move quickly up and down) — originally from mechanical switch engineering.

words.shortDefinition: Limiting the number of times a function can be called over a certain period.
words.longDefinition: Unlike debouncing, throttling guarantees that the function will be called at most once per specified time interval. It is used to provide a consistent rate of execution.
words.techContext: Essential for performance-heavy events that fire rapidly, such as 'scroll' or 'resize'.
words.exampleSentence: The scroll listener was causing major lag, so we throttled it to fire only once every 100ms.
words.etymology: from 'throttle' (to control the flow of fuel/power).

words.shortDefinition: A form of dead code elimination that removes unused code from a bundle.
words.longDefinition: A step in the build process where the bundler (like Webpack or Rollup) analyzes the dependency graph and excludes any exported functions or modules that are never actually imported or used.
words.techContext: Relies on the static structure of ES6 modules (import/export) to safely identify which code can be removed.
words.exampleSentence: After enabling tree shaking, our final bundle size dropped by 40% because we were only using two functions from the entire Lodash library.
words.etymology: Metaphor of shaking a tree so that only the dead leaves (unused code) fall off.

words.shortDefinition: The process of removing unnecessary characters from source code without changing its functionality.
words.longDefinition: Involves stripping out whitespace, comments, and newlines, as well as shortening variable and function names. This reduces the file size of JS, CSS, and HTML files sent over the network.
words.techContext: A standard step in production build pipelines, often handled automatically by tools like Terser or CSSNano.
words.exampleSentence: Minification and Gzip compression work together to ensure our production assets are as small as possible.

words.shortDefinition: Dividing a large bundle into smaller chunks that can be loaded on demand.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to split your code into various bundles which can then be loaded on demand or in parallel. It can be used to achieve smaller initial bundles and control resource load priority.
words.techContext: Often implemented via dynamic imports in React (using React.lazy) or Next.js to ensure the user only downloads the code for the page they are currently visiting.
words.exampleSentence: We used code splitting to move the admin dashboard logic into a separate bundle so regular users don't have to download it.

words.shortDefinition: The time it takes for a browser to receive the first byte of data from the server.
words.longDefinition: Measures the delay between the user making an HTTP request and the browser receiving the first byte of the response. It includes DNS lookup, connection time, and server-side processing time.
words.techContext: A high TTFB often indicates server-side bottlenecks, slow database queries, or a lack of edge caching.
words.exampleSentence: Our TTFB was over 800ms due to unoptimized database queries on the home page.
words.etymology: Descriptive of the measurement (Time to First Byte).

words.shortDefinition: The time from when the page starts loading to when any part of the page's content is rendered.
words.longDefinition: Measures the time until the browser renders the first bit of content from the DOM, providing the first feedback to the user that the page is actually loading. Content includes text, images, or non-white canvas elements.
words.techContext: A key user-centric performance metric. Fast FCP helps reassure users that the site is working.
words.exampleSentence: We improved our FCP by 30% after moving our CSS from an external file to an inline style block.

words.shortDefinition: The time it takes for the largest image or text block to become visible.
words.longDefinition: One of the three Core Web Vitals. It marks the point in the page load timeline when the page's main content has likely loaded. A good LCP is 2.5 seconds or less.
words.techContext: Typically triggered by a hero image or a large heading. Improving LCP usually involves optimizing server response time and resource load times.
words.exampleSentence: Optimizing the hero image's size and using a CDN helped us bring our LCP down to 1.8 seconds.

words.shortDefinition: The time from when a user first interacts with a page to when the browser begins processing that interaction.
words.longDefinition: Measures the 'responsiveness' of a page during load. It tracks the time between a user interaction (like clicking a button) and when the main thread is free enough to start processing that interaction.
words.techContext: High FID is usually caused by long-running JavaScript tasks that block the main thread during page load.
words.exampleSentence: Our FID was poor because a large third-party analytics script was hogging the main thread during initial boot.

words.shortDefinition: A measure of the visual stability of a page.
words.longDefinition: It quantifies how often users experience unexpected layout shifts—when elements move around while the page is loading. This often happens because images or ads load without dimensions and push other content down.
words.techContext: Calculated by summing the 'impact' and 'distance' fractions of all unexpected shifts. A score of less than 0.1 is considered good.
words.exampleSentence: Adding explicit width and height to our images eliminated the CLS that was annoying our mobile users.

words.shortDefinition: A metric that assesses a page's overall responsiveness to all user interactions.
words.longDefinition: Unlike FID (which only looks at the first interaction), INP observes the latency of all interactions throughout the entire life of a page. It represents the time from user interaction to the next frame being painted.
words.techContext: The successor to FID in Google's Core Web Vitals (as of 2024). It provides a more comprehensive view of interactivity than FID.
words.exampleSentence: We fixed a long-running click handler in our filter sidebar to improve the site's INP score.

words.shortDefinition: A geographically distributed group of servers that work together to provide fast delivery of Internet content.
words.longDefinition: A CDN allows for the quick transfer of assets needed for loading Internet content including HTML pages, javascript files, stylesheets, and images. It reduces latency by serving content from a server physically closer to the user.
words.techContext: Standard for any global application. Services like Cloudflare, Akamai, or AWS CloudFront are common examples.
words.exampleSentence: Moving our static assets to a CDN reduced load times for our European users by nearly 2 seconds.

words.shortDefinition: A hint to the browser to fetch a resource as soon as possible.
words.longDefinition: The <link rel='preload'> tag tells the browser to download a resource (like a font or a large script) with high priority because it will be needed very soon in the page load process.
words.techContext: Often used for custom web fonts to prevent 'Flash of Unstyled Text' (FOUT) or for critical CSS/JS files.
words.exampleSentence: I preloaded our primary brand font so it wouldn't be delayed by the execution of the main stylesheet.

words.shortDefinition: A hint to the browser to download resources that might be needed in future navigations.
words.longDefinition: Unlike preloading (which is for the current page), prefetching is a low-priority hint for resources that the user is likely to need in the *next* page they visit. The browser downloads these during idle time.
words.techContext: Useful for multi-page apps where you can guess the user's next step (e.g., prefetching the 'Check out' page while they are in the 'Cart').
words.exampleSentence: When the user hovers over a link, we start prefetching the data for that page to make the eventual transition feel instant.

words.shortDefinition: An open-source, automated tool for improving the quality of web pages.
words.longDefinition: A tool provided by Google (built into Chrome DevTools) that runs audits for performance, accessibility, progressive web apps, SEO, and more. It provides a score from 0-100 for each category.
words.techContext: The industry standard for measuring and verifying frontend performance improvements.
words.exampleSentence: The Lighthouse report suggested that we could save 1.2 seconds just by serving our images in WebP format.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'guiding light' to safety (quality).

words.shortDefinition: A modern compression algorithm optimized for the web.
words.longDefinition: Developed by Google, Brotli is a lossless data compression algorithm that generally provides better compression ratios than Gzip for text-based assets (HTML, CSS, JS).
words.techContext: Most modern browsers and servers support Brotli. It's especially effective because it uses a dictionary of common web-related strings.
words.exampleSentence: Switching from Gzip to Brotli saved an additional 15% on our main JavaScript bundle size.
words.etymology: Named after 'Brotli' (small bread) in Swiss German.

words.shortDefinition: The minimum set of CSS required to render the above-the-fold content of a page.
words.longDefinition: A technique where you identify the CSS needed for the content the user sees immediately on load and inline it directly in the <head> of the HTML, while loading the rest of the CSS asynchronously.
words.techContext: Drastically improves First Contentful Paint by removing the need for a separate network request before the page can start rendering.
words.exampleSentence: After we inlined the critical CSS, the page appeared to load instantly even on slow 4G connections.

words.shortDefinition: The third major version of the Hypertext Transfer Protocol.
words.longDefinition: Unlike previous versions that used TCP, HTTP/3 uses QUIC (a transport layer protocol based on UDP). It reduces latency and solves the 'head-of-line blocking' problem that can occur in HTTP/2.
words.techContext: Provides significant performance benefits for mobile users on unstable networks due to its faster connection establishment and better handling of packet loss.
words.exampleSentence: Our site feels much faster on mobile devices since our load balancer started supporting HTTP/3.
words.etymology: The third iteration of the HTTP standard.

words.shortDefinition: The process of delivering the smallest possible high-quality images.
words.longDefinition: Involves using modern formats (WebP, AVIF), proper sizing (srcset), and compression. Since images often account for the majority of a page's weight, this is one of the most effective ways to improve performance.
words.techContext: Automated via 'image proxies' or services like Cloudinary, which automatically serve the best format based on the browser's capabilities.
words.exampleSentence: Converting our PNG product photos to AVIF reduced their file size by 80% without any visible loss in quality.

words.shortDefinition: The process of pre-fetching and rendering a whole page in the background.
words.longDefinition: The browser effectively opens a hidden tab, downloads all resources, and fully renders the page. When the user eventually clicks the link, the transition appears instantaneous because the work is already done.
words.techContext: The most aggressive form of resource optimization. Should be used sparingly as it consumes significant bandwidth and battery if the user doesn't visit the page.
words.exampleSentence: We used the Speculation Rules API to prerender the next lesson in the course, making the 'Next' button feel like a local app transition.

words.shortDefinition: The process of encoding information using fewer bits than the original representation.
words.longDefinition: In web performance, this usually refers to 'HTTP Compression' (like Gzip or Brotli) where the server shrinks the file before sending it, and the browser expands it upon arrival.
words.techContext: A fundamental requirement for any web server. It drastically reduces the transfer time of text-based assets like HTML, CSS, and JS.
words.exampleSentence: Enabling Gzip compression on our Nginx server reduced the transfer size of our main bundle from 500KB to 120KB.

words.shortDefinition: Combining multiple source files into a single, optimized file.
words.longDefinition: The process of taking hundreds of small JavaScript or CSS files and combining them into a few 'bundles'. This reduces the number of HTTP requests a browser has to make, which was especially critical in the HTTP/1.1 era.
words.techContext: Performed by tools like Webpack, Vite, or Esbuild. Modern bundling also involves minification and code splitting.
words.exampleSentence: Our build process handles the bundling of all our modular components into a single 'main.js' file for production.
words.etymology: from 'bundle' (a collection of things held together).

words.shortDefinition: Storing copies of files in a temporary storage location for faster access.
words.longDefinition: Can occur at many levels: the browser's local cache, a CDN, or a server-side cache (like Redis). It avoids re-downloading or re-calculating the same data multiple times.
words.techContext: Proper 'Cache-Control' headers are the primary way to manage how long a browser should keep an asset before checking for a new version.
words.exampleSentence: We set a one-year cache header for our hashed CSS files since their filenames change whenever the content is updated.

words.shortDefinition: Sending multiple requests and responses in parallel over a single TCP connection.
words.longDefinition: A key feature of HTTP/2. Unlike HTTP/1.1, where requests had to be made one by one or via multiple connections, multiplexing allows many resources to be downloaded simultaneously over a single connection without blocking each other.
words.techContext: Eliminates the need for 'domain sharding' and makes bundling less critical than it used to be.
words.exampleSentence: Thanks to HTTP/2 multiplexing, our 50 small icon images load simultaneously without waiting for each other to finish.
